a: Athanasios Tasis is a Computer Engineering & Informatics student at the University of Patras, focused on scalable data systems, distributed systems, AI/ML, scientific computing and production-minded engineering.

He is best described as an early-career engineer with a data-engineering and systems profile: Python/C++ pipelines, HPC workflows, distributed-systems projects, applied machine learning and strong interest in AI infrastructure.
He builds CERN-scale data pipelines and AI-oriented systems, turning large noisy datasets into reliable outputs with Python, C/C++, Linux and distributed-systems thinking.
This assistant is not a general chatbot. It answers only about Athanasios Tasis, his professional background, projects, skills, work style, goals and contact channels.

He is studying for an integrated 5-year MEng/Diploma in Computer Engineering & Informatics at the University of Patras, a broad engineering program covering software, systems, networks, algorithms, databases and machine learning.

Relevant coursework includes Distributed Systems, Machine Learning, Information Retrieval, Computer Networks, Operating Systems, Algorithms & Data Structures, Databases and software-engineering-related subjects.
His diploma thesis topic is Dynamic Scaling of Computing Resources in Edge Computing using Reinforcement Learning. It connects edge computing, resource allocation and RL-based decision making.
The thesis explores how computing resources in edge environments can be scaled dynamically according to workload, performance and efficiency requirements using reinforcement-learning methods.

Athanasios worked as a Data Engineering Intern on CERN ALICE-related Time Projection Chamber quality-control workflows, hosted at GSI Helmholtz Centre for Heavy-Ion Research from February to May 2026.

q: what did he do at cern
a: His CERN/ALICE work involved automated ROOT/C++ and Python-oriented data-processing pipelines that transformed detector time-series outputs into quality-control plots and validation artifacts.
The work dealt with large detector-derived datasets, production HPC batch workflows, ROOT files, automated plotting, run-level processing and validation of outputs for monitoring detector behavior.
The practical value of the CERN/ALICE work was reducing manual inspection effort, standardizing outputs and making detector-quality monitoring more automated, reproducible and scalable.
The technical stack around the CERN/ALICE work included ROOT, C++, Python, Linux, HPC batch systems, shell scripting, file validation, plotting workflows and scientific-computing infrastructure.
His experience combines scientific data engineering, HPC workflows, teaching assistance, database labs, distributed-systems projects, ML/time-series work and full-stack academic projects.
He built a distributed hash-table engine implementing Chord and Pastry concepts, focusing on routing, data placement, parallel ingestion, simulated nodes and correctness under node churn.
The DHT project handled roughly 946K records and 300+ simulated nodes, validating O(log N)-style routing behavior and robustness under heavy node failure scenarios.
He built a TF-IDF search engine over a medical corpus with indexing, preprocessing, stemming, cosine ranking, multiple TF-IDF schemes, grid-search tuning and evaluation using MAP.
The search-engine project used a corpus of 1,239 articles and evaluated several ranking configurations, including a large grid search to tune retrieval quality.
He built a wireless-sensor-network forecasting pipeline for temperature, humidity and light, using real sensor data, cleaning steps, derived time features and forecasting/evaluation workflows.
The WSN dataset required timestamp normalization, duplicate handling, gap detection, interpolation, outlier checks and feature engineering before modeling.
The WSN project used time-series and ML approaches such as SARIMAX and regression baselines, evaluated with rolling or time-series cross-validation.
He built a thesis-management web platform covering thesis lifecycle workflows, role-based access, database triggers, audit logging and public JSON/XML REST endpoints.
His strongest projects are the CERN/ALICE data-processing work, the DHT distributed-systems engine, the WSN time-series forecasting pipeline and the TF-IDF search engine.

The most difficult project was likely the CERN/ALICE work because it combined unfamiliar scientific software, real detector data, HPC constraints, validation requirements and production-style expectations.
For data-engineering roles, the CERN/ALICE work and WSN forecasting pipeline are the most relevant because they involve data ingestion, cleaning, transformation, validation and reproducible outputs.
For distributed-systems roles, the DHT project is highly relevant because it demonstrates routing, fault tolerance, data placement and distributed algorithm implementation.
For ML/data-science roles, the WSN forecasting and TF-IDF projects are the most relevant because they include feature engineering, model evaluation, search quality and experimental comparison.
He works primarily with Python and C/C++, and also has experience with Java, PHP, SQL, JavaScript and Prolog.
Python is his main productivity language for data processing, ML workflows, automation, analysis scripts, ETL-style work and experimentation.
C/C++ is important in his profile for performance-sensitive systems, ROOT-based scientific computing and lower-level engineering work.
His ML/data tooling includes scikit-learn, PyTorch, TensorFlow, NumPy, pandas and statsmodels-style time-series workflows.

His data-engineering skills include ingestion, cleaning, transformation, validation, reproducibility, batch workflows, plotting/report generation and working with large structured or scientific datasets.
He has hands-on HPC exposure through batch-processing detector data, Linux-based environments, shell scripting, ROOT workflows and production-style scientific computing.
Linux is part of his practical toolkit for development, shell workflows, servers, HPC environments, debugging and automation.
His networking knowledge includes TCP/IP, routing, VLANs, OSPF, BGP, MPLS, firewalls, Cisco IOS, MikroTik and GNS3.
His distributed-systems skills include DHTs, peer-to-peer routing, distributed data placement, failure handling, concurrency and scalability analysis.
He debugs by reproducing issues, checking assumptions, isolating components, reading logs, validating inputs and comparing expected versus actual outputs.
He values testing and validation because pipeline outputs can look plausible while still being wrong. He prefers checks that catch corrupted files, missing data, leakage and inconsistent outputs.

His research interests include distributed systems, data engineering, machine learning, reinforcement learning, AI infrastructure, MLOps/AIOps, edge computing, scientific computing and time-series modeling.
He is interested in reinforcement learning especially when it controls real resource-allocation decisions, such as scaling compute in edge environments.
AI infrastructure interests him because it sits between ML, systems, deployment, data pipelines and reliability engineering.
Scientific computing interests him because it involves real-world data volume, correctness requirements and software that supports discovery rather than just business reporting.
His long-term goal is to work on large-scale distributed systems, AI infrastructure, data platforms and engineering problems where software, data and ML meet.
He is interested in data engineering, backend engineering, distributed-systems engineering, AI/ML engineering, MLOps/AIOps, scientific computing and early-career software engineering roles.
He is a strong data-engineering fit because his background includes data ingestion, transformation, validation, HPC processing, time-series pipelines, SQL, Python and scientific datasets.
He is an ML-engineering fit because he understands both model workflows and the surrounding data/validation infrastructure needed to make ML results reliable.
He is a strong fit for scientific-computing environments because he has already worked with detector-derived data, ROOT workflows, HPC constraints and reproducibility requirements.
His ideal company or lab solves hard technical problems at scale, values engineering depth and works with data, AI infrastructure, distributed systems, cloud platforms or scientific computing.
He is open to new opportunities after July 2026, especially internships, graduate roles and early-career engineering positions with serious technical depth.
He prefers to understand the system before changing it: reproduce the issue, inspect data, isolate variables, measure behavior and then implement the smallest reliable fix.
He is evidence-driven. He trusts logs, measurements, validation checks, plots and reproducible tests more than intuition alone.
He prefers owning complete technical problems from requirements and architecture through implementation, validation and delivery.
He learns best by building working systems, breaking them, debugging them and connecting the practical result back to the theory.

A realistic weakness is that he can spend too much time going deep into technical details. He manages this by defining deliverables, timeboxing exploration and separating research from production work.
His main strengths are persistence, curiosity, systems thinking, fast learning, technical depth and the ability to connect data, software and infrastructure.
He is motivated by hard technical problems, real-world impact, large-scale data, systems that must be correct and work that teaches him something nontrivial.
He values correctness, simplicity, reproducibility, observability, maintainability and measurable results.
Good code, in his view, is correct, readable, testable, observable and easy to change without breaking hidden behavior.

Hire him if you need an early-career engineer who can work across data pipelines, Python/C++, distributed systems, ML workflows and Linux/HPC environments with strong persistence and technical curiosity.
He stands out because his profile is not only coursework: he has hands-on CERN/ALICE scientific-computing experience, teaching experience and projects that cover systems, data and ML.
One of his proudest achievements is contributing to CERN/ALICE data-processing and quality-control automation because it combined real scientific data, HPC workflows and operational reliability.

He is a Computer Engineering & Informatics student with a strong focus on scalable data systems, distributed systems and AI/ML. His background includes CERN/ALICE data engineering, teaching assistance and projects in DHTs, forecasting and search.
He is drawn to data engineering because it combines software, systems, reliability and real-world data. He enjoys building the infrastructure that turns messy input into trusted output.
He likes distributed systems because they force engineers to think about scale, failure, consistency, routing and behavior that cannot be understood from one machine alone.
MLOps matters because models are only useful when data, evaluation, deployment, monitoring and rollback are engineered properly.
Testing is not only unit tests. For data systems it also means schema checks, file checks, distribution checks, leakage checks and output sanity checks.
He received the Eurobank Ergasias Scholarship, awarded to the top-ranked first-year student in his department.
Being selected for and completing CERN/ALICE-related data-engineering work at GSI is a major professional achievement in his early career.
